channel.title: Hapi — Happy News by Topic
channel.description: Uplifting stories from around the world, organised by topic.

item.title: Africa's Economy Set for 4.0% Growth, East Africa Racing Ahead at 5.8%
item.description: A new UN report forecasts Africa's economy will grow 4.0% in 2026 and 4.1% in 2027, with East Africa — led by Ethiopia and Kenya — surging ahead at 5.8%. Investments in renewable energy, digital infrastructure, and regional integration are driving the acceleration. Africa's working-age population and growing intra-continental trade are cementing its position as one of the world's most dynamic growth stories.

item.title: South America's Adventure Tourism Industry Races Toward a $40 Billion Boom
item.description: South America's adventure tourism sector is projected to reach nearly $40 billion by 2031, growing at a remarkable 15.3% annually. Brazil, Argentina, Chile, Colombia, and Peru are leading the charge as a record wave of international visitors discovers the continent's extraordinary biodiversity and cultural richness. The boom is injecting fresh economic momentum into communities from Patagonia to the Amazon.

item.title: Google and Stony Brook University Launch AI Academy for Long Island Businesses
item.description: Stony Brook University and the Long Island Association have launched a Google-funded AI Academy, equipping local small businesses with AI literacy skills and $5,000 implementation grants. The initiative is part of a broader national push to ensure the economic benefits of artificial intelligence reach communities well beyond Silicon Valley, turning local entrepreneurs into drivers of regional growth and job creation.

item.title: Artemis II Crew Shatters Record for Farthest Humans From Earth
item.description: NASA's Artemis II crew reached 252,757 miles (406,773 km) from Earth — more than 50,000 miles beyond the previous record set by Apollo — as they swept around the far side of the Moon. The four astronauts are the first humans to travel this deep into space in more than 50 years, marking a triumphant return to lunar exploration and reigniting wonder about humanity's future beyond our home planet.

item.title: New Neuro-Symbolic AI Cuts Energy Use by 100x While Improving Accuracy
item.description: Researchers have developed a neuro-symbolic AI architecture that uses 100 times less energy than conventional deep learning systems — while actually improving accuracy. The approach mimics human reasoning, breaking problems into logical steps rather than brute-forcing patterns. The breakthrough could make sophisticated AI viable on mobile devices and in low-energy environments, dramatically expanding where intelligent systems can be deployed.

item.title: Japanese Scientists Push Solar Panel Efficiency Beyond 130%
item.description: A Japanese research team has achieved solar cell efficiency of 130% — surpassing what was once considered a hard theoretical ceiling — using a multi-junction design that captures a broader slice of the light spectrum. The advance could fundamentally reshape the economics of solar power, requiring far less land and fewer panels to generate the same energy, with profound implications for the global clean energy transition.

item.title: High Seas Treaty Enters Into Force, Protecting International Ocean Biodiversity
item.description: The landmark High Seas Treaty has officially entered into force, creating the first-ever legal framework to protect biodiversity in international waters — which cover nearly half of Earth's surface. Nations can now establish marine protected areas, regulate bioprospecting, and share the benefits of ocean resources fairly. Scientists call it a historic turning point for ocean conservation, long overdue and globally transformative.

item.title: Monarch Butterfly Population Soars 64%, Occupying Most Land Since 2018
item.description: Mexico's monarch butterfly overwintering colonies grew 64% this season, now covering 7.24 acres — the largest area since 2018. The rebound follows years of milkweed restoration, reduced pesticide use, and trilateral conservation commitments between the United States, Canada, and Mexico. Researchers call it a hard-won win for one of the world's most iconic and beloved migratory species.

item.title: Portugal Powers 80.7% of Its Grid From Renewables — a Nine-Month Best
item.description: Portugal generated 80.7% of its electricity from renewable sources in January 2026 — the country's best renewable share in nine months and a powerful signal that its clean energy transition has reached a new baseline. Wind and hydro led the way, with solar contributing a growing share. Energy watchers say Portugal now consistently operates one of the greenest grids of any European nation.

item.title: US Smoking Rate Falls to Historic 9.9% — First Time in Single Digits
item.description: The share of US adults who smoke has dropped to 9.9% — the lowest ever recorded and the first time in history the figure has fallen below 10%. The milestone reflects decades of tobacco control policies, healthcare interventions, and cultural shifts around smoking. Public health experts say the number represents millions of lives saved and stands as one of the quiet triumphs of modern preventive medicine.

item.title: Chile Becomes the First Country in the Americas to Eliminate Leprosy
item.description: The World Health Organization has officially certified Chile as the first country in the Americas — and only the second globally — to eliminate leprosy as a public health problem. Sustained treatment programs, community health outreach, and early detection infrastructure achieved what was once seen as an ambitious long-term goal. Health officials say Chile's approach offers a replicable roadmap for the dozen countries still fighting the disease.

item.title: New Drug Zorevunersen Dramatically Reduces Seizures in Children With Dravet Syndrome
item.description: Early clinical trials of zorevunersen are showing life-changing promise for children with Dravet syndrome, a severe form of childhood epilepsy. Trial participants experienced significant reductions in seizure frequency, along with improvements in communication and quality of life. Researchers and families describe the results as transformative — offering real hope for a condition that has long resisted effective treatment.

item.title: France Opens One of Its First LGBTQIA+-Inclusive Senior Living Residences
item.description: A pioneering LGBTQIA+-inclusive senior residence has opened in Lyon, France — one of the first in the country. Designed as a space of safety, dignity, and true community, the facility ensures older LGBTQIA+ individuals can grow old without fear of discrimination. Advocates say it represents a meaningful step forward in eldercare and reflects a broader societal commitment to inclusion across every stage of life.

item.title: Colombia's Youth Choir to Perform at Walt Disney World and Rio de Janeiro
item.description: Colombia Canta y Encanta, a children's and youth choir from Medellín, has confirmed performances at Walt Disney World Resort on April 18 and in Rio de Janeiro later in the year. The group — ambassadors of Colombia's vibrant musical traditions — will share their culture and joy with audiences thousands of miles from home, celebrating what music can do when children lead the way.

item.title: 130+ Nations Sign Landmark Accord Protecting Jaguars, Manta Rays, and Migratory Birds
item.description: More than 130 governments signed a sweeping global wildlife protection accord at the COP15 migratory species conference in Brazil, granting stronger safeguards to jaguars, manta rays, and scores of migratory bird species. The agreement is the most expansive update to international wildlife law in years, and a powerful signal that governments are willing to act together for the species that connect ecosystems across continents and oceans.

item.title: Paddington The Musical Sweeps Olivier Awards With Four Wins
item.description: London's West End celebrated a joyful night as Paddington The Musical swept the 2026 Olivier Awards, winning Best New Musical, Best Director, Best Set Design, and Best Costume Design. The beloved Peruvian bear's theatrical debut captivated audiences with warmth and wonder, proving that a story about kindness, belonging, and a very good marmalade sandwich can take the stage by storm.

item.title: SWISH Festival Puts 200+ Indianapolis Artists in the Spotlight at Final Four
item.description: The SWISH Arts & Culture Festival brought together more than 200 local Indianapolis artists, performers, chefs, and vendors during the 2026 NCAA Men's Final Four, offering paid opportunities and a world-class stage for homegrown talent. The four-day festival featured live music, dance, visual art, and food — a joyful proof that big sporting events and thriving local arts scenes can lift each other up.

item.title: Super Mario Galaxy Movie Rockets to $629M Global Box Office in Two Weekends
item.description: The Super Mario Galaxy Movie has become a global phenomenon, earning $629 million worldwide in just two weekends of release. With $308 million at home and $321 million internationally, the film has already surpassed the first Mario Movie's opening pace. Audiences of all ages are cheering for the Mushroom Kingdom's latest cinematic adventure, reminding Hollywood that wholesome, joyful storytelling is box-office gold.

item.title: New Zealand's Stunning Te Kaha Stadium Opens in Christchurch
item.description: The NZ$683 million One New Zealand Stadium at Te Kaha has officially opened in Christchurch, offering a 30,000-person capacity for sports and 37,500 for concerts. The world-class venue replaces the earthquake-lost AMI Stadium and marks a triumphant chapter in Christchurch's long rebuilding story. International acts and sporting fixtures are already booked, and locals say the arena has breathed new life into the city's heart.

item.title: World Health Day Sports Events in Ashgabat Champion Youth and Gender Equality
item.description: A large-scale sports festival organized by UNFPA and Turkmenistan's Sports Committee filled the Olympic Village in Ashgabat on World Health Day, drawing young athletes to celebrate healthy living and gender equality through sport. Women's participation was central to the program, with coaches, officials, and young competitors sharing a day of movement, connection, and mutual inspiration rooted in the power of sport.

item.title: April 2026: A Golden Month for Sports Fans With Masters, Final Fours, and More
item.description: April 2026 is shaping up as one of the great months for sport, packed wall to wall with marquee events: The Masters, the NCAA Men's and Women's Final Fours, the NBA and NHL playoffs, WrestleMania, and the NFL Draft. Fans across North America are basking in a rare convergence of championships, and cities like Indianapolis and Augusta are alive with the electricity of high-stakes competition and shared celebration.

item.title: Africa Set to Be Among the World's Fastest-Growing Economies in 2026
item.description: A new UN report projects Africa's economy will grow 4.0% in 2026 and 4.1% in 2027, with several nations ranking among the world's fastest-growing. Capital is flowing in, the working-age population is expanding, and South-South trade is growing at 9% — nearly double the global average. Africa is consolidating its place at the center of the global development story.

item.title: South America Crowned the World's Top Tourism Destination for 2026
item.description: Brazil, Argentina, Peru, and Colombia are promoting South America as the world's premier travel destination for 2026, topping a global liveability ranking drawn from 24,000 resident surveys. A record international visitor wave is discovering the continent's extraordinary biodiversity, vibrant culture, and cuisine — injecting fresh momentum and sustained investment into regional economies.

item.title: Virginia's Giant Wind Farm to Save Customers $3 Billion Over a Decade
item.description: Dominion Energy's Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind project — the largest in the US — has started generating electricity while still 30% from completion. When fully built, it will power 660,000 homes and is projected to deliver $3 billion in customer savings over its first decade, demonstrating that large-scale offshore wind investment now pays economic dividends alongside its clean energy benefits.

item.title: Scientists Unlock a Million-Year Climate Archive From Antarctic Seafloor
item.description: CSIRO's RV Investigator completed a landmark Antarctic voyage, recovering a record 20.5-meter sediment core and over 10,000 samples from the Cook Ice Shelf. These geological time capsules encode climate data stretching back hundreds of thousands of years. The trove will help scientists build far more precise models of how Earth's frozen south responds to warming temperatures.

item.title: iNaturalist Turns 6 Million Nature-Lovers Into Contributing Scientists
item.description: The iNaturalist app has transformed 6 million monthly users into citizen scientists, generating biodiversity records and habitat change data now cited in peer-reviewed research worldwide. From backyards to rainforests, smartphone-toting enthusiasts are documenting species in real time, filling critical gaps in scientific knowledge and showing that a love of nature can be a genuine contribution to global science.

item.title: Uzbekistan and ZTE Partner to Build a 6G-Ready Digital Economy
item.description: Uzbekistan's Ministry of Digital Technologies signed a landmark MOU with ZTE Corporation to accelerate digital transformation across the country. The agreement covers high-speed broadband, advanced data centers, and next-generation 6G and Wi-Fi 8 technologies. The partnership positions Uzbekistan as a growing digital hub in Central Asia and opens new economic pathways for the broader region.

item.title: Chile Creates 337,000 km² No-Take Marine Reserve Around Juan Fernández
item.description: Chile's President Boric signed a decree establishing one of the world's largest no-take marine reserves, protecting 337,000 square kilometers around the Juan Fernández archipelago and placing more than 50% of Chile's waters under conservation. The Juan Fernández fur seal — once near extinction — has rebounded to 200,000 individuals, proof that sustained protection can reverse even the steepest declines.

item.title: UK Renewable Energy Tops 52% for Second Straight Year — a New Normal
item.description: Great Britain's renewables generated 52.5% of its electricity in 2025 — a new record and the second consecutive year above 50%. Wind power was the dominant contributor. In March 2026, record renewable output saved the UK nearly £1 billion in gas imports. Clean energy has moved from aspiration to dependable baseline, redefining what is possible for an industrialized nation's grid.

item.title: African Conservation Models Revive Gorillas and Restore Watersheds
item.description: Across Africa, community-led conservation is delivering measurable results. In Cameroon, a women's initiative cut farmer-herder conflicts by 62%. Tanzania communities are restoring river ecosystems through indigenous tree planting. Mountain gorilla populations have grown significantly, with eco-lodges now earning millions for local communities — proving that when communities lead conservation, both nature and prosperity follow.

item.title: WHO: Global Maternal Mortality Down 40%, Child Deaths Halved Since 2000
item.description: Marking World Health Day 2026, the WHO highlighted sweeping global health gains: maternal mortality has fallen by more than 40% since 2000, and deaths among children under five have been reduced by more than 50%. Nearly 800 scientific institutions from 80+ countries are now collaborating on health breakthroughs that are turning once-deadly conditions into manageable diseases.

item.title: Staying Optimistic Linked to 15% Lower Dementia Risk, Study Finds
item.description: A landmark 14-year study of over 9,000 cognitively healthy adults found that people with higher optimism scores had approximately 15% lower risk of developing dementia. Researchers believe optimism protects the aging brain through reduced stress hormones, healthier lifestyle habits, and stronger social connections — adding compelling evidence to the growing science of hope and long-term brain health.

item.title: Dogs Detect Parkinson's With 98% Accuracy Years Before Symptoms Appear
item.description: Dogs trained to detect Parkinson's disease through scent are achieving 98% accuracy — identifying the condition years before clinical symptoms emerge. The breakthrough could transform early diagnosis, enabling patients to begin protective treatment far sooner. Researchers believe this approach could meaningfully slow disease progression and improve quality of life for millions of people worldwide.

item.title: Elbas Transform Sierra Leone's Sherbro Island From Aid to Investment
item.description: Actor Idris Elba and wife Sabrina are reshaping the future of Sherbro Island, Sierra Leone, shifting the region's story from aid dependency to sustainable investment. Their initiative pairs ecological preservation with enterprise development, drawing international attention and funding. National Geographic reports the project is already opening new economic pathways for a community long overlooked by global markets.

item.title: PBS Kids' First Autistic Lead Character Wins Emmy for Public Service
item.description: Carl the Collector, PBS Kids' groundbreaking show featuring its first autistic lead character, earned an Emmy Award for Outstanding Public Service Initiative. The joyful series portrays a child navigating the world on his own wonderful terms. Families and educators report it has sparked meaningful conversations about neurodiversity, belonging, and what it means to see yourself reflected on screen.

item.title: California's Pet-Friendly Shelters Are Keeping Homeless Families Together
item.description: California's $15 million investment to make homeless shelters welcoming to pets is showing real results, removing the critical emotional barrier of having to leave animals behind. Early outcomes show more people successfully transitioning off the streets, keeping human and animal families intact, and rebuilding the stability needed for long-term housing security.

item.title: China Opens Immersive Sound Museum in Reclaimed Industrial Ruins
item.description: An abandoned factory in China has been reborn as the 1Y Sound Museum, where walls of reclaimed materials broadcast oral histories from former workers and local residents. Designed by 1Y Architects, the space transforms industrial ruins into a living archive of human experience — where the sounds of the past create meaningful dialogue with the communities of today.

item.title: Stephen Colbert Raises Over $1 Million for World Central Kitchen
item.description: Late Night host Stephen Colbert mobilized his audience to raise over $1 million for World Central Kitchen through a T-shirt campaign and live on-air auction. More than 33,000 fans purchased shirts, and auction proceeds added another $400,000. The campaign is a testament to how entertainment can turn laughter and loyalty into life-changing generosity for people in crisis.

item.title: The 'Slow Looking' Movement Is Changing How We Experience Art
item.description: The 'slow looking' movement — spending extended, meditative time with a single artwork — is gaining global momentum in galleries worldwide. Studies show the practice significantly boosts wellbeing and emotional depth, countering the typical three-second gallery glance. Advocates call it a gentle antidote to the culture of speed: staying with one piece reveals layers that rushed viewing can never uncover.

item.title: UK Dad Founds Forget-Me-Knot FC: Football for Bereaved Fathers
item.description: After losing a child, one British father created Forget-Me-Knot FC — a football club exclusively for dads who have experienced child bereavement. Starting with just six members, the team has grown to 36, finding solidarity, healing, and joy through the beautiful game. Plans are underway to expand the club across the UK, turning personal grief into a nationwide community of support.

item.title: Women's Sports Revenues Are Surging at Double-Digit Rates Globally
item.description: Deloitte's 2026 Global Sports Industry Outlook reveals women's sports commercial revenues are growing at double-digit rates worldwide, with media rights, sponsorships, and fan engagement all reaching new heights. Alongside record franchise valuations and stadium development, women's sports are fundamentally reshaping global athletics — proving beyond any doubt that investing in women's games pays dividends in every sense.

item.title: Vietnam's First Sports Economy Summit Opens Billion-Dollar Conversations
item.description: Vietnam hosted its inaugural Sports Economy Summit 2026, gathering government officials, investors, and industry leaders to map the sector's economic potential. Discussions spanned infrastructure, media rights, tourism linkages, and youth development. Experts say the summit marks a pivotal moment in how Vietnam — and broader Southeast Asia — thinks about sport as a serious and scalable economic driver.
